risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After confirming the diagnosis of uncomplicated AA by a CT scan, patients will be randomized by a closed envelope method either to undergo appendectomy or to receive antibiotic therapy with intravenous ertapenem. The randomization is performed in 1:1 equal allocation ratio. The 610 opaque, sealed, and sequentially numbered randomization envelopes are mixed and distributed to research hospitals by the main research center according to each hospital district population. To randomize a patient, an independent surgeon on duty will open the next consecutively numbered envelope." Comment: This describes a randomized allocation procedure using opaque, sealed, sequentially numbered envelopes opened in order by an independent surgeon, which is a proper randomization process and supports truly random sequence generation; no non-random rule is described.

_evidence.method_quote: “After confirming the diagnosis of uncomplicated AA by a CT scan, patients will be randomized by a closed envelope method either to undergo appendectomy or to receive antibiotic therapy with intravenous ertapenem. The randomization is performed in 1:1 equal allocation ratio. The 610 opaque, sealed, and sequentially numbered randomization envelopes are mixed and distributed to research hospitals by the main research center according to each hospital district population. To randomize a patient, an independent surgeon on duty will open the next consecutively numbered envelope.”
_evidence.method_summary: Patients were randomized 1:1 using opaque, sealed, sequentially numbered envelopes opened in order by an independent surgeon.
context_clues: opaque, sealed, and sequentially numbered randomization envelopes

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The 610 opaque, sealed, and sequentially numbered randomization envelopes are mixed and distributed to research hospitals by the main research center according to each hospital district population. To randomize a patient, an independent surgeon on duty will open the next consecutively numbered envelope." Comment: This explicitly describes a sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelope procedure (SNOSE) and states that the next envelope is opened only at the time of randomization, which prevents foreknowledge of the upcoming assignment.

_evidence.concealment_quote: The 610 opaque, sealed, and sequentially numbered randomization envelopes are mixed and distributed to research hospitals by the main research center according to each hospital district population. To randomize a patient, an independent surgeon on duty will open the next consecutively numbered envelope.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "prospective randomized controlled, open label, non-inferiority multicenter trial" and "the concealment would not be possible and therefore a randomized open design was chosen" Comment: Participants and treating personnel were explicitly not blinded in this open-label trial. Several key outcomes were subjective or could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation, including pain scores (VAS), use of pain medication, sick leave, treatment costs, and treatment success, so the lack of blinding creates a clear risk of performance bias.

_evidence.blinding_quote: prospective randomized controlled, open label, non-inferiority multicenter trial
_evidence.blinding_method: none; "the concealment would not be possible and therefore a randomized open design was chosen"

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The APPAC trial has been designed as a prospective randomized controlled, open label, non-inferiority multicenter trial" and "Patient outcome will be obtained during hospital stay (days 0, 1, 2) and then by a phone interview at one week, two months and at and one, three, five and ten years after the intervention." Comment: The study is explicitly open label, so outcome assessment was not blinded. The outcome set includes patient-reported and judgement-dependent outcomes, especially pain scores (VAS), sick leave, and the phone-interviewed follow-up outcomes, which are vulnerable to knowledge of allocation. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of these subjective outcomes makes detection bias high even though some outcomes (e.g. CT-based diagnosis, complications, recurrence) are more objective.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: The APPAC trial has been designed as a prospective randomized controlled, open label, non-inferiority multicenter trial to compare antibiotic therapy (intravenous ertapenem) with emergency appendectomy in the treatment of uncomplicated appendicitis.

outcomes.label: pain scores (VAS) and pain medication use

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The provided text states only that "The main analyses will be based on the intention-to-treat principle, but both intention-to-treat and per-protocol analyses will be performed" and that "With an estimated 10 percent of the trial patients lost to follow-up, a maximum of 610 patients will be enrolled." Comment: These are planned analyses and expected attrition, not observed post-randomization losses on the analysed outcomes. No CONSORT flow, per-arm randomized versus analysed numbers, or reasons for missing outcome data are reported in the supplied text, so the extent of attrition bias cannot be determined.

_evidence.notes: Protocol states: 'The main analyses will be based on the intention-to-treat principle, but both intention-to-treat and per-protocol analyses will be performed.' No participant flow numbers or attrition counts are reported in the provided text.
